turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the opinion of the Court in case 17-1174, Nieves v. Bartlett. Some of you may have heard of the Annual Burning Man gathering. This case involves an Alaskan festival that is the polar opposite, Arctic Man. Arctic Man is a winter sports event held in a remote mountain range near Paxson, Alaska. Paxson is normally a quiet hamlet with a few dozen residents, but once a year during Arctic Man, upwards of 10,000 people descend on the area for a week of extreme snow machine races, bonfires, and extensive alcohol consumption. On the last night of Arctic Man 2014, two police officers, who are the petitioners here, arrested respondent Russell Bartlett. Bartlett had run-ins with the police that night and according to the officers both times Bartlett was highly intoxicated and belligerent. The officers gave him a pass the first time, but on the second occasion they arrested him after he continued yelling at one of the officers and then stepped close toward him in a combative way. Now, Bartlett disputes that account. He says that he was not drunk at that time and that the officers treated him unreasonably. He also claims that the officers really arrested him because they disliked his speech, a violation of the First Amendment. The protected speech, according to Bartlett, was his refusal to talk with one of the officers during the first episode earlier that night. Bartlett claims that after his arrest the officer said, I bet you wished you would have talked to me now. Now, the State of Alaska eventually decided not to prosecute Bartlett and he then sued the officers claiming that they had violated his First Amendment rights by arresting him in retaliation for his speech. We agreed to hear this case to decide whether a person can sue a police officer for a First Amendment retaliatory arrest when the arresting officer had probable cause to believe the person was committing a crime; in other words, a valid reason for arresting him. We conclude that such claims fail as a matter of law. The First Amendment generally forbids government officials from taking retaliatory action against someone based on that person's protected speech. This Court has previously addressed such claims in a variety of civil context, like cases where a public employee claims he was fired because of his speech or where a person claims his public benefits were cut off for that reason. But this retaliation case arises in the law enforcement context. In the past we have treated retaliation cases in this general context somewhat differently. We have said that when law enforcement officers have an objectively reasonable basis to take an action against someone, that is probable cause, then that person generally cannot sue the officers for retaliation and that is the rule we adopt today for retaliatory arrests. In this country, police officers make 29,000 arrests a day. Conducting an arrest is dangerous and it requires officers to make split second decisions in situations that are tense, uncertain and rapidly evolving. To ensure that officers can go about this work without an undue fear of being sued, we generally review their conduct under objective standards of reasonableness. If we were to allow lawsuits like Bartlett's to proceed based solely on allegations about a particular officer's real motives even when the officer's decision to conduct an arrest had a reasonable basis, then officers would be vulnerable to suit virtually every time they made an arrest, and because this kind of motive-based claim is particularly hard to disprove, an officer's in artful turn of phrase during a legitimate arrest could land him in years of litigation. So the rule we adopt is that if an officer has probable cause to arrest an individual for a crime, the individual cannot sue him on the ground that the real reason was retaliation for speech. But there is an exception. There are many things people can be arrested for, but almost never are, like jaywalking. If you are jaywalking and say something a policeman does not like or are wearing a shirt with a message he does not like and he arrests you, he may have perfectly clear probable cause, no doubt about it you were jaywalking. But we do not think it would be sufficiently protective of the First Amendment to say that probable cause in a case like that defeats a retaliatory arrest claim. We think if you can show that people mostly are not arrested for what you were doing, even though it is against the law, you can proceed with your First Amendment claim. Bartlett's claim against the officers however is nothing of that sort. Far from being a jaywalker, there was an objectively reasonable basis to believe that Bartlett was intoxicated, belligerent, and directly threatening a police officer. That will get you arrested anywhere, even at Arctic Man. The judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit is reversed and the case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Thomas has filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment. Justice Gorsuch has filed an opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part. Justice Ginsburg has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment in part and dissenting in part, and Justice Sotomayor has filed a dissenting opinion.
